topic: Transformer architecture improvements in large language models 2020-2024
final_review:
The Transformer architecture has undergone significant improvements in recent years, particularly in the context of large language models (Gu and Dao, 2023; Gao et al., 2023). One of the key areas of research has been the development of more efficient and scalable models, such as the Mamba model, which achieves linear-time sequence modeling with selective state spaces (Gu and Dao, 2023). This has been complemented by research on retrieval-augmented generation for large language models, which has been surveyed by Gao et al. (2023).

Another area of research has been the application of large language models to specific domains, such as chemistry (Bran et al., 2024) and medicine (Singhal et al., 2025). For example, Bran et al. (2024) have demonstrated the potential of large language models in chemistry, while Singhal et al. (2025) have explored their use in medical question answering. The use of large language models in medicine has also been explored in the context of clinical text summarization, where adapted models have been shown to outperform medical experts (Van Veen et al., 2024).

Furthermore, research has been conducted on the role of AI in hospitals and clinics, with a focus on transforming healthcare in the 21st century (Varnosfaderani and Forouzanfar, 2024). In terms of explainability, large language models have been surveyed by Zhao et al. (2024), who highlight the need for more transparent and interpretable models. This is particularly important in high-stakes domains such as medicine, where the consequences of errors can be severe (Zhao et al., 2024; Wang et al., 2024).

There are also some contradictions and research gaps in the literature. For example, while some studies have demonstrated the potential of large language models in medicine (Singhal et al., 2025), others have highlighted the need for more rigorous evaluation and testing (Van Veen et al., 2024). Additionally, there is a need for more research on the ethical implications of using large language models in healthcare (Varnosfaderani and Forouzanfar, 2024).

In conclusion, the Transformer architecture has undergone significant improvements in recent years, with a focus on efficiency, scalability, and domain-specific applications. However, there are still many research gaps and contradictions in the literature, particularly in the context of medicine and healthcare. Further research is needed to fully realize the potential of large language models in these domains.

References:
Albert Gu, T. D. (2023). Mamba: Linear-Time Sequence Modeling with Selective State Spaces. https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2312.00752
Andres M. Bran, S. C., & Oliver Schilter. (2024). Augmenting large language models with chemistry tools. https://doi.org/10.1038/s42256-024-00832-8
Dave Van Veen, C. V. U., & Louis Blankemeier. (2024). Adapted large language models can outperform medical experts in clinical text summarization. https://doi.org/10.1038/s41591-024-02855-5
Haiyan Zhao, H. C., & Fan Yang. (2024). Explainability for Large Language Models: A Survey. https://doi.org/10.1145/3639372
K. K. Singhal, T. T., & Juraj Gottweis. (2025). Toward expert-level medical question answering with large language models. https://doi.org/10.1038/s41591-024-03423-7
Lei Wang, C. M., & Xueyang Feng. (2024). A survey on large language model based autonomous agents. https://doi.org/10.1007/s11704-024-40231-1
Shiva Maleki Varnosfaderani, & Mohamad Forouzanfar. (2024). The Role of AI in Hospitals and Clinics: Transforming Healthcare in the 21st Century. https://doi.org/10.3390/bioengineering11040337
Yunfan Gao, Y. X., & Xinyu Gao. (2023). Retrieval-Augmented Generation for Large Language Models: A Survey. https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2312.10997
